title: Record magnetoresistance, enhanced superconductivity, and fermiology in WTe2
authors: Gianluca Delgado, Elliott Runburg, Chaowei Hu, Yuzhou Zhao, Jonathan M. DeStefano, Keng Tou Chu, Florie Mesple, Ellis Thompson, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Jihui Yang, Matthew Yankowitz, Xiaodong Xu, Jiun-Haw Chu, David H. Cobden
abstract: The diverse electronic properties of transition metal chalcogenides can be very sensitive to crystal imperfections. A new crystal growth technique, known as horizontal flux transport, offers a route to improved crystal quality. By refining this technique and applying it to the topological semimetal WTe2, we achieved crystals with an order of magnitude less disorder as determined by electrical transport and scanning tunneling microscopy measurements. At low temperatures these crystals exhibit the largest magnetoresistance reported in a metal. Exfoliated monolayers show quantum oscillations for the first time in the electrostatically doped metallic states, enabling determination of band degeneracies and the valley splitting induced by an electric field. Moreover, they exhibit a gated superconducting dome with a greatly enhanced critical temperature approaching 1.8 K. This advance opens up new avenues for employing WTe2 in topological electronics and gated superconducting devices, and promises comparable breakthroughs with other chalcogenides.
summary: This paper reports a breakthrough in the growth of WTe2 using a Horizontal Flux Transport technique, which significantly reduces crystal disorder. This improved quality allows for the observation of unprecedentedly large magnetoresistance and the first quantum oscillations in WTe2 monolayers. Furthermore, the researchers demonstrate an enhanced superconducting dome with a critical temperature reaching 1.8 K. These findings provide a new platform for studying topological electronics and gated superconducting devices.
main_problem: The electronic properties of WTe2 are highly sensitive to crystal disorder, which obscures intrinsic phenomena like quantum oscillations and superconductivity.
main_result: Using a new Horizontal Flux Transport growth technique, the authors achieved record-high crystal purity, leading to the largest reported magnetoresistance in a metal, enhanced superconductivity (Tc up to 1.8 K), and the first observation of quantum oscillations in WTe2 monolayers.
method: The study employs Horizontal Flux Transport (HFT) for crystal growth, Scanning Tunnelability Microscopy (STM) for defect characterization, and low-temperature electrical transport measurements (including Shubnikov-de Haas oscillations) in dual-gate field-effect transistors.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of high-purity bulk and monolayer WTe2, a topological semimetal, studied in both bulk crystals and electrostatically doped monolayer field-effect transistors encapsulated by hBN.
key_observables: Residual Resistivity Ratio (RRR), magnetoresistance (MR), transport and quantum mobility, critical temperature (Tc), valley splitting, and Landau fan diagrams.
important_parameters: RRR up to 12,200; magnetoresistance of 7.8 x 10^5 at 13 T; Tc approaching 1.8 K; electron density (ne) and perpendicular displacement field (D_perp).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the superconductivity follows BCS clean-limit theory and that the discrepancy between transport and quantum mobility is due to persistent long-range scattering mechanisms.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares HFT vs conventional growth and shows STM images; Figure 2 displays resistance maps showing Landau fans and valley splitting; Figure 3 presents evidence of the superconducting dome and scaling of critical current.
paper_structure: The paper begins by introducing the HFT growth method and demonstrating improved crystal quality, followed by bulk transport properties (magnetoresistance), then moves to monolayer studies (quantum oscillations and valley splitting), and concludes with the characterization of the enhanced superconducting state and defect analysis.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Horizontal flux transfer (HFT) growth and characterization of WTe2 crystals. a. Binary phase diagram of WTe2 contrasting growth techniques. In conventional self-flux growth, a starting solution of W in Te is steadily cooled from temperature 𝑇𝑖 to 𝑇𝑓 and the crystals form as the saturated solution tracks the solubility line (green). In HFT growth, the source dissolves at 𝑇1 (black star) and crystals grow at a constant temperature 𝑇2 and flux composition (black dot). b. (Top) sketch of how this is achieved, by applying a temperature gradient to a tilted sealed ampule with a neck which traps the source metal at higher temperature 𝑇1, where it gradually dissolves, while crystals grow...

Figure 2. Quantum oscillations in monolayer WTe2 at high magnetic fields. a. 4-terminal longitudinal resistance 𝑅𝑥𝑥 vs gate-doping 𝑛𝑒 and perpendicular displacement field 𝐷⊥ at 𝐵= 9 T for device 1. The combs are drawn to show how the fundamental period of the SdH oscillations (spacing of the thicker lines) on the electron-doped side (right) is twice that on the hole-doped side (left). Upper inset: cartoon of monolayer device structure. Lower inset: Brillouin zone and sketch of bands along the zone axis at 𝐵= 0. b. 𝑅𝑥𝑥 vs 𝑛𝑒 and 𝐵 at 𝐷⊥= 0. A single Landau fan dominates, with minima indicated by the dashed lines, and evidence for spin degeneracy lifting is visible at higher fields (dotted...

Figure 3. Enhanced gated superconductivity. a. 𝑅𝑥𝑥 vs gate doping 𝑛𝑒 and displacement field 𝐷⊥ at 𝐵= 0 and base temperature. b. 𝑅𝑥𝑥 vs 𝑛𝑒 at 𝐷⊥= 0 for the indicated temperatures. Inset: 𝑅𝑥𝑥 vs 𝑇 at the indicated dopings. Solid lines are guides for the eye. c. Critical temperature 𝑇𝑐 vs 𝑛𝑒 (black circles) for Device 1, and corresponding measurements from three monolayer WTe2 devices reported in the literature (red diamonds [Ref. 33], blue triangles [Ref. 14], green squares [Ref. 15]). All solid lines here are guides for the eye. d. Variation of critical field 𝐵𝑐 with 𝑛𝑒 at the indicated temperatures. Left inset: a trace of 𝑅𝑥𝑥 vs 𝐵 at 100 mK and 𝑛𝑒= 4.6 × 1012 cm-
